mappings.rationaleAI: Elizabeth I's reign (1558-1603) including the Elizabethan Religious Settlement, struggle with Spain, defeat of the Armada, and cultural flowering directly addresses Elizabeth I's domestic policies, dynastic challenges, and struggle with Spain (A.5.d.iii).

The Spanish Empire's Habsburg expansion, silver mining, colonial economy, and rivalry with Portugal directly address Spain from 1516 to 1665 (A.5.b), establishment of colonial empires (B.1.b), and colonial expansion's political effects including Spain's status as an international power (B.4).

The Protestant Reformation's theological challenges, wars of religion, and Catholic Counter-Reformation directly address the emergence of religiously divided Europe (A.3), Protestant Reformation (A.3.a), Catholic Reformation (A.3.b), and Luther's origins and the Diet of Worms (A.5.f.i).
The Holy Roman Empire's Reformation, Counter-Reformation, Habsburg-Hohenzollern rivalry, and Thirty Years' War directly address Germany and the Holy Roman Empire from c. 1500 to 1648 (A.5.f).

The British Empire's origins in Caribbean and North American colonies and rivalry with France relate to establishment of colonial empires (B.1.b), colonial rivalries (B.4.b), and the Seven Years' War (C.1.b.ii).

The Renaissance's humanism, political theory, visual arts, and spread from Italy directly address the later Renaissance in Italy and northern Europe (A.1), Machiavelli and statecraft (A.1.a), and cultural and intellectual life (A.1.b).

The early modern period (c. 1500-1800) including Renaissance, Reformation, Age of Discovery, mercantile capitalism, Scientific Revolution, and Enlightenment comprehensively addresses religious and cultural change (A), European overseas expansion (B), and France and Great Britain as dominant powers (C).

Suleiman's military campaigns into Central Europe and conflicts with the Habsburgs in Hungary relate to French and Austrian struggles for supremacy and French anti-Habsburg alliances with the Turks (A.4.b).
The American Revolution relates to Franco-Spanish cooperation against Britain in the U.S. War of Independence (C.1.b.iii) and the failure of George III's colonial policies (C.2.a.iii).
The Age of Discovery's maritime exploration and colonial empires directly address the beginning of European imperialism (B.1) and voyages of discovery and establishment of colonial empires (B.1.b).

The Enlightenment's emphasis on reason, individual liberty, and rational reform directly addresses the age of the Enlightenment (C.3), including origins in 17th-century scientific achievements and developments in political and religious philosophies (C.3.a).
France's history from Capetian monarchy through the 1789 Revolution directly addresses France from 1483 to 1643 (A.5.e) and France from c. 1650 to c. 1790 (C.2.b).
Italy's Renaissance heritage and early modern political fragmentation directly address Italy in the 16th and 17th centuries under Spanish domination (A.5.a) and Italy in the 18th century (C.2.f).
John Locke's empiricism, social contract theory, and natural rights directly relate to origins of the Enlightenment in 17th-century developments in political and religious philosophies (C.3.a).
The Ottoman Empire's conflicts with Catholic powers relate to conflicts between Catholic and Protestant powers after c. 1555 including the Ottoman conflict (A.4.c).
The Atlantic slave trade's triangular route and economic impact directly address the development and importance of the slave trade (B.4.a.v).
The United Kingdom's history from Tudor through Hanoverian succession directly addresses the British Isles from c. 1485 to 1649 (A.5.d) and Great Britain from 1649 to c. 1790 (C.2.a).
Columbus's four voyages under Spanish sponsorship directly relate to discovery, exploration, and early settlement of the Americas (B.1.b.i).
Galileo's contributions to astronomy and physics and his championing of heliocentrism directly relate to the Scientific Revolution and emergence of modern science (A.2).
European history's coverage of Renaissance, Reformation, mercantilism, colonial expansion, Scientific Revolution, and Enlightenment provides broad context across all major outline sections.
Spain's Habsburg and Bourbon periods directly address Spain from 1516 to 1665 (A.5.b) and Spain from 1665 to c. 1790 under Charles II and the Bourbons (C.2.e.i).
Martin Luther's Ninety-five Theses, justification by faith alone, and Bible translation directly address the Protestant Reformation (A.3.a) and Luther's origins and the Diet of Worms (A.5.f.i).

Descartes' foundational role in the Scientific Revolution and modern philosophy relates to the emergence of modern science (A.2) and origins of the Enlightenment in 17th-century scientific achievements (C.3.a).

The Industrial Revolution's origins in Britain relate to technological advances and pre-industrial manufacturing systems (B.3) and origins of the Industrial Revolution and factory system in British economic developments (C.2.a.iv).
Imperialism's extension of power over foreign territories relates to the beginning of European imperialism and rapid expansion of European trade and control over the non-European world (B.1).
European colonial empires employing mercantilism and chartered companies relate to European overseas expansion and commercial development (B) and the impact of colonial expansion on Europe (B.4).
The Mughal Empire's European maritime trade integration relates to discovery, exploration, and early settlement of coastal regions of India (B.1.b.ii).
Adam Smith's critique of mercantilism and theory of free markets in The Wealth of Nations relates to Enlightenment origins in developments in political and religious philosophies (C.3.a).
Poland's partitions by Russia, Prussia, and Austria relate to the Russian-Prussian partition of Poland (1772) (C.1.b.iii).
Europe's overview of Renaissance, Reformation, Enlightenment, and colonial expansion provides broad context across all major outline sections.
